expanded_description: Right triangle trigonometry is the foundation of trigonometry—defining sine, cosine, and tangent using right triangle ratios (SOH CAH TOA). This connects the unit circle approach from previous sections to the more traditional right triangle approach used in applications. In right triangles, trig functions are ratios of sides: sin = opposite/hypotenuse, cos = adjacent/hypotenuse, tan = opposite/adjacent. The reciprocal functions (csc, sec, cot) are reciprocals of these ratios. Special right triangles (30-60-90 and 45-45-90) have exact side ratios that give exact trig values—these are essential for exact calculations. Solving right triangles means finding all missing sides and angles—this is fundamental to surveying, navigation, engineering, and physics. Applications include angles of elevation/depression (looking up/down), bearings (navigation directions), and indirect measurement (finding heights you can't measure directly). In Pre-calculus, mastering right triangle trigonometry is essential for solving trigonometric equations, verifying trigonometric identities, and preparing for calculus applications. The right triangle approach complements the unit circle approach—both define the same trig functions, just from different perspectives. Understanding both builds complete understanding of trigonometry. This section teaches right triangle trig—definitions, special triangles, solving triangles, applications, and connections to unit circle.

core_concept.principle:
Right Triangle Trig Definitions:
For acute angle θ in right triangle:

sin θ = opposite/hypotenuse (SOH)
cos θ = adjacent/hypotenuse (CAH)
tan θ = opposite/adjacent (TOA)

Reciprocal Functions:
csc θ = 1/sin θ = hypotenuse/opposite
sec θ = 1/cos θ = hypotenuse/adjacent
cot θ = 1/tan θ = adjacent/opposite

Mnemonic: SOH CAH TOA
Sine = Opposite/Hypotenuse
Cosine = Adjacent/Hypotenuse
Tangent = Opposite/Adjacent

Special Right Triangles:
45°-45°-90°: sides 1 : 1 : √2
30°-60°-90°: sides 1 : √3 : 2

Solving Right Triangles:
Given enough information (2 sides, or 1 side + 1 angle), find all missing sides and angles using:
- Trig ratios (sin, cos, tan)
- Pythagorean theorem (a² + b² = c²)
- Angle sum (angles add to 180°)
core_concept.explanation: Think of right triangle trigonometry as ratios—sin, cos, tan are just ratios of sides in a right triangle. SOH CAH TOA is the mnemonic to remember which ratio is which. For any acute angle in a right triangle, you can find these ratios. The beauty is it works for ANY right triangle—similar triangles have same ratios, so the ratios depend only on the angle, not the triangle size. Special triangles (30-60-90 and 45-45-90) have exact side ratios—you should memorize these because they give exact trig values (no decimals). Solving right triangles means finding everything—you're given some information, use trig to find the rest. This is practical—surveyors use this to measure heights, navigators use this to find positions, engineers use this to design structures. The right triangle approach connects to the unit circle—the unit circle is just a right triangle with hypotenuse 1. Understanding both approaches builds complete understanding. This section teaches right triangle trig—ratios, special triangles, solving, and applications.

image.alt: Overview of right triangle trigonometry showing SOH CAH TOA and special triangles
image.caption: Figure 7.6.1: Visual summary of right triangle trigonometry: SOH CAH TOA definitions, special right triangles (45-45-90 and 30-60-90), and solving right triangles.

sections.section_title: 7.6.1 Right Triangle Trig Ratios (SOH CAH TOA)
sections.introduction: In a right triangle, the trigonometric functions are defined as ratios of sides. For an acute angle θ, the sides are: opposite (across from θ), adjacent (next to θ), and hypotenuse (opposite the right angle). The three primary trig functions are sine, cosine, and tangent, defined by the mnemonic SOH CAH TOA.

introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.6.2: Introducing SOH CAH TOA mnemonic for right triangle trig ratios.

key_concept.definition:
Right Triangle Sides:
For acute angle θ in right triangle:
- Opposite: side across from angle θ
- Adjacent: side next to angle θ (not hypotenuse)
- Hypotenuse: side opposite right angle (longest side)

Primary Trig Functions:

sin θ = opposite/hypotenuse (SOH)
cos θ = adjacent/hypotenuse (CAH)
tan θ = opposite/adjacent (TOA)

Reciprocal Trig Functions:

csc θ = 1/sin θ = hypotenuse/opposite
sec θ = 1/cos θ = hypotenuse/adjacent
cot θ = 1/tan θ = adjacent/opposite

Mnemonic: SOH CAH TOA
Pronounced 'so-kah-toh'
Most common mnemonic for remembering trig ratios

Key Properties:
- Ratios depend only on angle, not triangle size
- Similar triangles have same trig ratios
- For complementary angles (add to 90°):
sin(90° - θ) = cos θ
cos(90° - θ) = sin θ
tan(90° - θ) = cot θ
key_concept.context: SOH CAH TOA is fundamental to trigonometry—it's how trig was originally defined (before unit circle). In applications, this is what you use for right triangle problems—surveying, navigation, engineering. The reciprocal functions (csc, sec, cot) appear in calculus (derivatives of trig functions) and physics (certain formulas use them). Understanding that ratios depend only on angle (not triangle size) is key—this is why trig works for similar triangles. The complementary angle relationships (sin(90°-θ) = cos θ) are useful for simplifying expressions. This section teaches SOH CAH TOA—the foundation of right triangle trigonometry.

image.caption: Figure 7.6.3: Diagram showing right triangle with sides labeled (opposite, adjacent, hypotenuse) and SOH CAH TOA ratios.

worked_examples.title: Finding Trig Ratios from Right Triangle

image.alt: Example finding trig ratios from right triangle
image.caption: Figure 7.6.4: Finding all six trigonometric ratios from a right triangle.

worked_examples.problem: For the right triangle with opposite = 3, adjacent = 4, hypotenuse = 5, find all six trig functions for angle θ.

step_by_step.explanation: For angle θ: opposite = 3, adjacent = 4, hypotenuse = 5.

worked_examples.concept_applied: SOH CAH TOA: sin=opp/hyp, cos=adj/hyp, tan=opp/adj; reciprocals for csc, sec, cot.

worked_examples.title: Finding Missing Side Using Trig Ratios

image.alt: Example finding missing side using trig
image.caption: Figure 7.6.5: Using trigonometric ratios to find missing side of right triangle.

worked_examples.problem: In a right triangle, angle θ = 30°, hypotenuse = 10. Find the opposite side.

step_by_step.explanation: Have hypotenuse, need opposite. Use sine (SOH).

worked_examples.concept_applied: Choose correct trig ratio based on known and unknown sides; use known trig values to solve.

worked_examples.title: Finding Missing Angle Using Inverse Trig

image.alt: Example finding missing angle using inverse trig
image.caption: Figure 7.6.6: Using inverse trigonometric functions to find missing angle.

worked_examples.problem: In a right triangle, opposite = 3, adjacent = 4. Find angle θ.

step_by_step.explanation: Given: opposite = 3, adjacent = 4. Find: angle θ.
Have opposite and adjacent. Use tangent (TOA).

worked_examples.concept_applied: Use inverse trig functions to find angles: θ = tan⁻¹(opp/adj), sin⁻¹(opp/hyp), or cos⁻¹(adj/hyp).

image.caption: Figure 7.6.7: Summary of SOH CAH TOA and right triangle trig ratios.

sections.introduction: Special right triangles (45°-45°-90° and 30°-60°-90°) have exact side ratios that give exact trigonometric values. These triangles appear frequently in trigonometry, and their exact values should be memorized. They provide exact answers (no decimals) for common angles (30°, 45°, 60°).

introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.6.8: Introducing special right triangles (45-45-90 and 30-60-90).

key_concept.definition:
45°-45°-90° Triangle:
Isosceles right triangle (two 45° angles)
Side ratios: 1 : 1 : √2
Legs are equal, hypotenuse = leg × √2

Trig values:
sin 45° = cos 45° = √2/2
tan 45° = 1

30°-60°-90° Triangle:
Side ratios: 1 : √3 : 2
Shortest side (opposite 30°) = 1
Longer leg (opposite 60°) = √3
Hypotenuse = 2

Trig values:
sin 30° = 1/2, cos 30° = √3/2, tan 30° = √3/3
sin 60° = √3/2, cos 60° = 1/2, tan 60° = √3

Memory Tips:
45-45-90: legs same, hypotenuse has √2
30-60-90: shortest side opposite 30°, hypotenuse is 2× shortest

Why Memorize:
- Give exact values (no decimals)
- Appear frequently in trig and calculus
- Faster than calculator for common angles
key_concept.context: Special triangles are fundamental—they give exact trig values for 30°, 45°, 60°. In calculus, you need exact values for many problems (limits, derivatives, integrals). In physics, exact values are often required for symbolic solutions. In engineering, exact values are used for precise calculations. Memorizing these triangles saves time—you don't need calculator for common angles. The side ratios come from geometry—45-45-90 from square cut diagonally, 30-60-90 from equilateral triangle cut in half. Understanding the geometry helps remember the ratios. This section teaches special triangles—their side ratios and trig values.

image.caption: Figure 7.6.9: Diagram showing 45-45-90 and 30-60-90 triangles with side ratios and trig values.

worked_examples.title: Using 45°-45°-90° Triangle

image.caption: Figure 7.6.10: Using 45-45-90 triangle to find exact trig values.

worked_examples.problem: Use the 45°-45°-90° triangle to find exact values of sin 45°, cos 45°, and tan 45°.
worked_examples.concept_applied: 45-45-90 triangle: sides 1:1:√2; use SOH CAH TOA to find trig values.

worked_examples.title: Using 30°-60°-90° Triangle

image.caption: Figure 7.6.11: Using 30-60-90 triangle to find exact trig values.

worked_examples.problem: Use the 30°-60°-90° triangle to find exact values of sin 30°, cos 30°, sin 60°, and cos 60°.
worked_examples.concept_applied: 30-60-90 triangle: sides 1:√3:2; use SOH CAH TOA to find trig values for 30° and 60°.

worked_examples.title: Finding Exact Trig Values Without Calculator

image.caption: Figure 7.6.12: Finding exact trigonometric values using special triangles.

worked_examples.problem:
Find exact values (no calculator):
a) tan 60°
b) sin 45°
c) cos 30°
worked_examples.concept_applied: Use special triangles for exact values; rationalize denominators when needed.

image.caption: Figure 7.6.13: Summary of special right triangles and exact trig values.

sections.introduction: Solving a right triangle means finding all missing sides and angles. Given enough information (two sides, or one side and one acute angle), you can find everything using trig ratios, Pythagorean theorem, and angle sum (angles add to 180°). This is fundamental to surveying, navigation, engineering, and physics.

introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.6.14: Introducing solving right triangles—finding all missing sides and angles.

key_concept.definition:
Solving Right Triangle:
Find all missing sides and angles.

Given Information:
Case 1: Two sides → find third side (Pythagorean), find angles (inverse trig)
Case 2: One side + one acute angle → find other side (trig ratios), find other angle (angle sum)

Tools:
- Pythagorean theorem: a² + b² = c²
- Trig ratios: sin, cos, tan (SOH CAH TOA)
- Inverse trig: sin⁻¹, cos⁻¹, tan⁻¹
- Angle sum: acute angles add to 90°

Strategy:
1. Label triangle (identify known/unknown)
2. Choose appropriate tool (Pythagorean, trig, inverse trig)
3. Solve for unknown
4. Find remaining parts
5. Check answer (makes sense?)

Important:
- Use exact values when possible
- Round appropriately for applications
- Check that answers are reasonable
key_concept.context: Solving right triangles is practical—surveyors use this to measure land, navigators use this to find positions, engineers use this to design structures. In physics, this is used for vector components, projectile motion, forces. The process is systematic—given some info, use appropriate tools to find the rest. Pythagorean theorem finds missing sides, trig ratios find sides from angles, inverse trig finds angles from sides. Understanding when to use which tool is key. This section teaches solving right triangles—the process, tools, and strategies.

image.caption: Figure 7.6.15: Diagram showing process for solving right triangles with different given information.

worked_examples.title: Solving Right Triangle Given Two Sides

image.alt: Example solving right triangle given two sides
image.caption: Figure 7.6.16: Solving right triangle when given two sides.

worked_examples.problem: Solve the right triangle with legs a = 3, b = 4. Find hypotenuse c and angles A and B.

step_by_step.explanation: Use Pythagorean theorem: a² + b² = c².

worked_examples.concept_applied: Given two sides: Pythagorean for third side, inverse trig for angles, angle sum for remaining angle.

worked_examples.title: Solving Right Triangle Given Side and Angle

image.alt: Example solving right triangle given side and angle
image.caption: Figure 7.6.17: Solving right triangle when given one side and one angle.

worked_examples.problem: Solve the right triangle with hypotenuse c = 10 and angle A = 30°. Find sides a, b and angle B.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Given side and angle: angle sum for other angle, trig ratios for other sides, verify with Pythagorean.

worked_examples.title: Complete Solution with All Steps

image.caption: Figure 7.6.18: Complete solution of right triangle showing all steps.

worked_examples.problem: Solve the right triangle with a = 7, A = 40°. Find b, c, and B.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Complete solution: angle sum for angle, trig ratios for sides, verify with Pythagorean.

image.caption: Figure 7.6.19: Summary of solving right triangles with different given information.

sections.introduction: Right triangle trigonometry has numerous real-world applications—angles of elevation/depression (looking up/down), bearings (navigation directions), indirect measurement (finding heights you can't measure directly), and more. These applications show why trigonometry matters—it solves real problems in surveying, navigation, engineering, and physics.

introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.6.20: Introducing applications of right triangle trigonometry.

key_concept.definition:
Angles of Elevation/Depression:
Angle of elevation: angle looking up from horizontal
Angle of depression: angle looking down from horizontal
Both measured from horizontal line

Bearings:
Direction measured from north or south
N 30° E: 30° east of north
S 45° W: 45° west of south

Indirect Measurement:
Find heights/distances you can't measure directly
Use trig ratios with known angle and distance

Common Applications:
- Surveying: measure land, heights of buildings
- Navigation: find positions, distances
- Engineering: design structures, forces
- Physics: projectile motion, vector components
- Astronomy: distances to stars, planets

Problem-Solving Strategy:
1. Draw diagram
2. Label known/unknown
3. Identify right triangle
4. Choose appropriate trig ratio
5. Solve for unknown
6. Check answer (reasonable?)
key_concept.context: Applications show why we learn trigonometry—it solves real problems. Surveyors use trig to measure land without walking every boundary. Navigators use trig to find positions at sea. Engineers use trig to design bridges, buildings, machines. Physicists use trig for forces, motion, waves. Understanding applications builds appreciation for trigonometry as practical tool, not just abstract math. The problem-solving strategy (draw, label, identify, choose, solve, check) works for all trig applications. This section teaches applications—angles of elevation/depression, bearings, indirect measurement, and problem-solving strategy.

image.caption: Figure 7.6.21: Diagram showing applications: angles of elevation/depression, bearings, indirect measurement.

worked_examples.title: Angle of Elevation Application

image.caption: Figure 7.6.22: Using angle of elevation to find height of building.

worked_examples.problem: From a point 50 feet from a building, the angle of elevation to the top is 60°. Find the height of the building.

step_by_step.explanation: Draw right triangle with angle of elevation.
step_by_step.calculation:
Distance from building: 50 ft (adjacent)
Angle of elevation: 60°
Height: h (opposite, unknown)

step_by_step.explanation: Have adjacent, need opposite. Use tangent.

step_by_step.calculation: 86.6 ft is reasonable for building height

worked_examples.concept_applied: Angle of elevation: draw right triangle, use tan = opp/adj to find height.

worked_examples.title: Angle of Depression Application

image.caption: Figure 7.6.23: Using angle of depression to find distance.

worked_examples.problem: From a cliff 100 meters high, the angle of depression to a boat is 25°. Find the distance from the base of the cliff to the boat.

step_by_step.explanation: Draw right triangle with angle of depression.
step_by_step.calculation:
Cliff height: 100 m (opposite)
Angle of depression: 25°
Distance: d (adjacent, unknown)

step_by_step.explanation: Angle of depression = angle of elevation (alternate interior angles).
Have opposite, need adjacent. Use tangent.
Multiply by d, divide by tan 25°.

step_by_step.calculation: 214.5 m is reasonable distance from cliff

worked_examples.concept_applied: Angle of depression: draw right triangle, angle of depression = angle of elevation, use tan to find distance.

worked_examples.title: Bearing Application

image.caption: Figure 7.6.24: Using bearings to find position in navigation.

worked_examples.problem: A ship sails 100 km on a bearing of N 60° E. How far north and how far east does it travel?

step_by_step.explanation: Draw right triangle with bearing angle.
step_by_step.calculation:
Hypotenuse: 100 km
Angle from north: 60°
North distance: n (adjacent)
East distance: e (opposite)

step_by_step.explanation: Use cos = adj/hyp for north component.
Use sin = opp/hyp for east component.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Bearings: draw right triangle, use sin/cos to find components (north/south, east/west).

image.caption: Figure 7.6.25: Summary of right triangle trigonometry applications.

sections.section_title: 7.6.5 Common Errors and How to Avoid Them
sections.introduction: Right triangle trigonometry introduces specific pitfalls related to choosing wrong trig ratio, calculator mode (degrees vs. radians), misidentifying sides, and application setup errors. Recognizing common mistakes helps students develop careful habits and produce accurate results. Understanding where errors typically occur enables self-correction and better problem-solving.

introduction_image.alt: Introduction to right triangle trig errors
introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.6.26: Overview of common errors in right triangle trigonometry.

key_concept.definition:
Common Errors:

1. Wrong Trig Ratio:
- Using sin when should use cos or tan
- Not matching ratio to known/unknown sides
- Forgetting SOH CAH TOA

2. Calculator Mode Error:
- Calculator in radians when should be degrees
- Getting wrong values for sin 30°, etc.
- Not checking calculator mode

3. Misidentifying Sides:
- Confusing opposite and adjacent
- Not identifying hypotenuse correctly
- Wrong side for given angle

4. Application Setup Errors:
- Wrong diagram for problem
- Misidentifying angle of elevation/depression
- Wrong trig ratio for application

5. Rounding Errors:
- Rounding too early in calculations
- Wrong number of significant figures
- Not checking if answer is reasonable

Error Prevention:
- Use SOH CAH TOA mnemonic
- Check calculator mode (DEG vs. RAD)
- Label triangle clearly (opp, adj, hyp)
- Draw accurate diagram for applications
- Keep extra digits during calculation, round at end
- Check if answer makes physical sense
key_concept.context: Right triangle trig errors are common—wrong trig ratio gives wrong answer. Calculator mode errors (degrees vs. radians) give completely wrong values. Side misidentification (opp vs. adj) gives wrong ratio. Application setup errors (wrong diagram) lead to wrong setup. Rounding errors accumulate and give inaccurate answers. Understanding these errors develops trig literacy—knowing which ratio, which mode, which sides. In applications, errors mean wrong heights, wrong distances, wrong positions. This section teaches error awareness—critical for accurate trig work.

image.caption: Figure 7.6.27: Diagram showing common errors in right triangle trigonometry and how to avoid them.

worked_examples.title: Correcting Wrong Trig Ratio Error

image.alt: Example correcting wrong trig ratio error
image.caption: Figure 7.6.28: Correcting errors in choosing trigonometric ratio.

worked_examples.problem: A student used sin to find adjacent side when given hypotenuse and angle. Identify and correct the error.

step_by_step.calculation:
Student used sin = opp/hyp
But needed adjacent side

step_by_step.explanation: For adjacent and hypotenuse, use cosine.

step_by_step.calculation:
cos θ = adj/hyp
adj = hyp × cos θ
Use cosine (CAH) when given hypotenuse and need adjacent

worked_examples.final_answer: Error: used sin instead of cos; Correct: use cos = adj/hyp (CAH)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Choose correct trig ratio based on known and unknown sides: SOH CAH TOA.

worked_examples.title: Correcting Calculator Mode Error

image.caption: Figure 7.6.29: Correcting errors from wrong calculator mode.

worked_examples.problem: A student calculated sin 30° and got 0.5 (correct) but sin 45° gave -0.524. Identify and correct the error.

step_by_step.explanation: Calculator is likely in radians mode.

step_by_step.calculation: Set calculator to DEG mode for degree problems

worked_examples.final_answer: Error: calculator in RAD mode; Correct: set to DEG mode for degree problems
worked_examples.concept_applied: Always check calculator mode (DEG vs. RAD) matches problem (degrees vs. radians).

worked_examples.title: Correcting Application Setup Error

image.caption: Figure 7.6.30: Correcting errors in application problem setup.

worked_examples.problem: A student solved angle of depression problem but used angle at ground instead of angle at top. Identify and correct the error.

step_by_step.calculation:
Student used angle at ground
Angle of depression is at top (looking down)

step_by_step.explanation: Angle of depression is measured from horizontal at top.
step_by_step.calculation: Angle of depression at top of cliff/building

Angle at top = angle of depression
Alternate interior angle at bottom = same measure
Angle of depression is at top; use alternate interior angle or draw from top

worked_examples.final_answer: Error: used wrong angle location; Correct: angle of depression is at top (looking down)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Angle of elevation/depression: draw accurate diagram, identify correct angle location.

image.caption: Figure 7.6.31: Summary of common errors in right triangle trigonometry.

sections.section_title: 7.6.6 Comprehensive Practice with Right Triangle Trigonometry
sections.introduction: Apply all skills related to right triangle trigonometry—SOH CAH TOA, special triangles, solving triangles, applications, and error avoidance—to demonstrate mastery. These problems integrate multiple concepts and prepare students for advanced trigonometry and applications.

worked_examples.title: Comprehensive Practice Set

image.caption: Figure 7.6.32: Practice problems covering right triangle trigonometry.

worked_examples.problem:
Complete the following problems:

1) sin = ?/hyp (SOH)
2) cos = adj/? (CAH)
3) tan = opp/? (TOA)
4) sin 30° = ?
5) cos 45° = ?
6) tan 60° = ?
7) 45-45-90 sides: 1 : 1 : ?
8) 30-60-90 sides: 1 : ? : 2
9) Solve triangle: a=3, b=4, find c.
10) Solve triangle: c=10, A=30°, find a.
11) Angle of elevation 45°, distance 50m, find height.
12) Angle of depression 30°, height 100m, find distance.
13) Bearing N 30° E, 100km, find north component.
14) csc = 1/?
15) sec = 1/?
16) cot = 1/?
17) sin²θ + cos²θ = ?
18) Calculator mode for degrees?
19) opp=5, hyp=10, find sin θ.
20) adj=3, opp=4, find tan θ.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Comprehensive practice demonstrating mastery of right triangle trigonometry—SOH CAH TOA, special triangles, solving, applications.

image.caption: Figure 7.6.33: Detailed solutions to all practice problems.

summary: Right triangle trigonometry: SOH CAH TOA—sin=opp/hyp, cos=adj/hyp, tan=opp/adj. Reciprocals: csc=1/sin, sec=1/cos, cot=1/tan. Special triangles: 45-45-90 (sides 1:1:√2), 30-60-90 (sides 1:√3:2). Special angle values: sin 30°=1/2, cos 30°=√3/2, tan 60°=√3, etc. Solving right triangles: given two sides (Pythagorean for third, inverse trig for angles) or one side + one angle (trig ratios for sides, angle sum for angles). Applications: angles of elevation/depression (looking up/down), bearings (navigation), indirect measurement (heights/distances). Common errors: wrong trig ratio, calculator mode (DEG vs. RAD), misidentifying sides, application setup errors. Use SOH CAH TOA mnemonic, check calculator mode, label triangle clearly, draw accurate diagrams. Essential for surveying, navigation, engineering, physics. Foundation for advanced trigonometry and calculus applications.

review_questions_image.alt: Comprehensive summary: right triangle trigonometry, SOH CAH TOA, special triangles, solving triangles, applications, common errors
review_questions_image.caption: Figure 7.6.34: Complete reference for right triangle trigonometry showing SOH CAH TOA, special triangles with side ratios, special angle values, solving process, and applications.
